input_path: data\papers\Zech et al. - Model-driven Digital Twins for AECO.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The physical system is a research‑grade Living Lab building used as a testbed for infrastructure digital twinning. It represents a full‑scale, operational building that houses typical building services (HVAC, lighting, power distribution, environmental sensors) and is instrumented with IoT devices to generate runtime data throughout its life‑cycle. The Living Lab’s heterogeneous subsystems and the need for real‑time data make it a suitable candidate for a Digital Twin implementation in the AECO domain.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin provides real‑time monitoring of building parameters, simulation of “what‑if” scenarios, predictive analytics for energy and comfort optimisation, and life‑cycle‑encompassing optimisation of building operations. It offers visualization dashboards for stakeholders, decision‑support analytics to enhance operational decisions, and control‑loop capabilities that can feed back optimisation set‑points to the physical system.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: Implementation relies on a model‑driven framework that integrates Eclipse Hono for ingestion of device telemetry, Eclipse Ditto for digital‑twin state management and real‑time data exchange, and Unreal Engine 5 for high‑fidelity, interactive visualisation of the building model. These components constitute the core technical enablers for data handling, twin synchronisation, and user interaction.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The framework keeps the digital twin in live synchronization with the physical asset by using Eclipse Hono for real‑time ingestion of sensor data and Eclipse Ditto for immediate propagation of state changes to the twin model. Updates are applied as soon as data are received, supporting real‑time monitoring and simulation; the document does not specify explicit latency values or update frequencies, and the temporal granularity follows the underlying sensor sampling rate.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The system follows a model‑driven, service‑oriented constellation. A conceptual model defines the DT components and their interactions. Eclipse Hono is used as the IoT connectivity layer for ingesting real‑time telemetry, while Eclipse Ditto provides the digital twin management backend (state persistence, property updates, command handling). The 3D visualisation and simulation layer is realised with Unreal Engine, which consumes the twin state via standardized APIs. Coordination between these components is achieved through well‑defined extension points and REST/WS‑based interfaces, allowing independent scaling and resource allocation of each service.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: External integration is realised through Eclipse Hono for standardized, protocol‑agnostic data exchange (e.g., MQTT, HTTP) and Eclipse Ditto for interoperable twin state exposure (JSON‑LD, WebSocket). The framework offers standardized extension points that enable plug‑in of additional enterprise systems such as BIM platforms or facility‑management solutions, adhering to open interoperability standards for data models and communication.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The framework enriches BIM models with semantic extensions that represent building control system (BCS) components—sensors, actuators, and controllers—creating a unified geometric‑behavioral model. Model‑Driven Engineering (MDE) extracts these enriched BIM artifacts into structured representations that are automatically compiled into deployable Digital Twin artifacts. Real‑time data from the physical devices are streamed into the twin via a continuous synchronization layer, forming the data backbone for analytics and interactive visualization.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: Actuators are captured as device entities inside the enriched BIM model and are linked to controller logic authored by the Building Control Engineer. The Digital Twin exposes a remote control interface through which Facility Managers can issue actuation commands that are translated into signals for the physical actuators. The model defines actuation capability but does not specify concrete actuator families, actuation ranges, or safety constraints.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: Sensors are modeled as semantic objects within the BIM environment, including type (e.g., temperature, occupancy) and spatial location. During operation, the Twin’s real‑time data exchange layer collects measurements from these physical sensors, synchronizes them with the digital counterpart, and makes the data available to visualization and control services. Specific sensor specifications such as sampling frequency, accuracy, or data transmission rates are not detailed in the document.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: The proposed approach includes a validation step in which the generated Digital Twin is assessed against the physical building using the Overall Digital Twin Entanglement (ODTE) metric. This metric quantifies how closely the twin reflects the real‑world asset, thereby providing a fidelity measure. Model validation is performed after the MDE‑driven generation of artifacts, and any discrepancies identified by the ODTE evaluation trigger corrective actions to improve model accuracy. The framework thus incorporates systematic verification of both structural (BIM‑derived) and dynamic (real‑time data) aspects of the twin to ensure high quality and trustworthy operation.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: The framework continuously synchronizes the physical building with its digital twin by streaming live operational sensor and device data into the DT. Real‑time data exchange is realized through the Eclipse Hono service for device connectivity and Eclipse Ditto for twin state management. Data are transmitted in a continuous (real‑time) fashion, triggered by any change in the physical devices. Incoming measurements (e.g., temperature, humidity, occupancy, actuator statuses) are pre‑processed by the central hub, validated for consistency, and then merged with the enriched BIM model via the framework’s standardized extension points.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: Bidirectional communication is supported: the DT Framework can issue control commands from the digital twin back to the building control systems, enabling Facility Managers to monitor and actuate physical infrastructure in real time. The document mentions the exchange of control signals but does not detail command formats, validation/authentication mechanisms, conflict resolution, or explicit confirmation of command execution.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: Communication between physical devices and the digital twin relies on the Eclipse Hono protocol stack for device ingestion and Eclipse Ditto for twin state synchronization, forming a central‑hub architecture. The modular, service‑oriented design supports interoperability across heterogeneous systems. Network requirements such as bandwidth, latency, redundancy, and security protocols are not explicitly quantified in the document, although the non‑functional requirements emphasize scalability, reliability, and security.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: Deployment follows a model‑driven approach where the DT Framework serves as a central hub, supporting modular components that can be scaled from individual devices to whole infrastructure systems. The framework allows low‑overhead updates and runtime reconfiguration, but the document does not specify the hosting environment (cloud, edge, on‑premise), computational or storage sizing, specific scalability mechanisms, deployment models, or availability/reliability guarantees.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages: The framework supports the full Digital Twin life‑cycle, starting with device modelling by Building Control Engineers directly inside the BIM environment (definition of sensors, actuators and controllers), followed by a model‑driven transformation step that uses Model‑Driven Engineering (MDE) to extract a structured representation and automatically generate deployable DT artefacts. The generated twin is then deployed, synchronised in real time with the physical building, and operated by Facility Managers through interactive visualisation. Runtime operation is complemented by a modular architecture with standardized extension points that allow DT Engineers to add custom services and analytics, enabling continuous evolution of the twin after deployment.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: Engineering follows a model‑driven approach: engineers model devices in BIM, enrich the BIM model with control logic, and apply MDE transformations to produce a formal DT description. The artefacts are automatically instantiated, minimising manual coding. The architecture provides standardized extension points for plugins, so DT Engineers can develop and integrate custom services. While the document does not detail explicit QA, testing or version‑control mechanisms, the use of MDE and modular extensions implies systematic generation and controlled evolution of the twin.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: The twin offers continuous, real‑time data exchange that keeps the digital and physical assets synchronised, and provides immersive interactive visualisation for Facility Managers to monitor building states and issue control commands. Decision support is therefore delivered through live visual feedback and direct control actions; however, specific analytics, confidence metrics or recommendation engines are not described in the document.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
I. I NTRODUCTION


Digital Twins (DT) are cyber-physical information constructs that through carefully designed integration of static
models with runtime data creates a virtual representation
of a (cyber-)physical asset (e.g., a building) for simulation, analysis, and optimization [1], [2]. Crucially, in the
architecture, engineering, construction and operations (AECO)
domain, DTs enable data-driven infrastructure management
and optimization by providing real-time insights, predictive
analytics, and enhanced decision-making capabilities [3]–[7].
This transformation promises tangible benefits such as reduced
operational costs, enhanced decision-making, and improved
stakeholder collaboration along the complete building life
cycle, from initial design to refurbishments and adaptations
during operation [2], [8]–[12]. However, the realization of this
potential substantially hinges on the ability to systematically
and efficiently deploy DTs in complex and heterogeneous
infrastructure environments [11]–[13].
Despite their promise, the deployment of infrastructure DTs
remains elusive in practice [2]. This is primarily due to
the fragmented nature of models and data, domain-specific


This research has received funding from the Austrian Research Promotion
Agency (FFG) under Grant Agreement No.: 898708, TwinLight, and Grant
Agreement No.: 910225, BOREALIS.

It accurately describes it as an operational testbed generating real-time data. While the text explicitly supports the mention of lighting systems and environmental conditions, the specific inclusion of HVAC and power distribution is a broad inference from 'building operations' and 'infrastructure,' though generally consistent with the AECO context described. Supported by quotes: 'demonstrate the practical application of our approach in a Living Lab case study', 'model-driven framework for scalable DT deployment... infrastructure', 'optimization of building operations'.

The extraction is highly accurate and comprehensive, covering all major services mentioned in the text: monitoring, simulation, predictive analytics, life-cycle optimization, visualization, and control. The description aligns perfectly with the documented capabilities of the Digital Twin framework. Supported by quotes: 'enabling real-time monitoring, simulation, and life cycle-encompassing optimization', 'providing real-time insights, predictive analytics, and enhanced decision-making capabilities', 'feed control signals back to the BCS', 'Unreal Engine for rich, interactive visualization'.

The extraction is highly specific and directly grounded in the source text. It correctly identifies the core technologies: 'Eclipse Hono', 'Eclipse Ditto', and 'Unreal Engine'. The description of their roles matches the text: Hono and Ditto for 'real-time data exchange' ('integrates Eclipse Hono and Ditto for real-time data exchange') and Unreal Engine for 'interactive visualisation' ('leverages Unreal Engine for rich, interactive visualization'). The mention of a 'model-driven framework' is also explicitly supported.

models with runtime data creates a virtual representation
of a (cyber-)physical asset (e.g., a building) for simulation, analysis, and optimization [1], [2]. Crucially, in the
architecture, engineering, construction and operations (AECO)
domain, DTs enable data-driven infrastructure management
and optimization by providing real-time insights, predictive
analytics, and enhanced decision-making capabilities [3]–[7].
This transformation promises tangible benefits such as reduced
operational costs, enhanced decision-making, and improved
stakeholder collaboration along the complete building life
cycle, from initial design to refurbishments and adaptations
during operation [2], [8]–[12]. However, the realization of this
potential substantially hinges on the ability to systematically
and efficiently deploy DTs in complex and heterogeneous
infrastructure environments [11]–[13].
Despite their promise, the deployment of infrastructure DTs
remains elusive in practice [2]. This is primarily due to
the fragmented nature of models and data, domain-specific

block_3_docs_retrieved:
TABLE I: Actor Roles in our Model-driven DT Deployment
Framework


**Role** **Description**


Building Control Engineer Models and configures devices, defines the control logic, and initiates
DT deployment from BIM models.
Facility Manager Monitors system states, interacts
with the twin, and operates the
physical infrastructure via the DT.
DT Engineer Develops and integrates custom services and plugins to extend DT
functionality.


Commensurate with the limitations of BIM as a seamless

enabler for DTs, we have developed a comprehensive set of
requirements for our model-driven deployment framework.
These requirements emerge from both theoretical considerations and practical insights gained through our research
projects, TwinLight and BOREALIS (cf. Section II).
To address the gap between BIM’s static representations and
the dynamic nature of DTs, our proposal must support the
complete life cycle of DT deployment and operation. Table II
outlines the use cases to be supported by our framework,
spanning from initial device modeling to the development
of custom DT services atop our proposal. These use cases
reflect the core functionality needed by different stakeholders
(cf. Table I) involved in the DT life cycle, including _Building_
_Control Engineers_ who model devices and implement control
logic, _Facility Managers_ who monitor and control building operations, and _DT Engineers_ who extend functionality through
custom services.

The functional requirements ( **F** _X_ ) detailed in Table III form
the conceptual foundation of our solution, beginning with
device modeling capabilities that address BIM’s fundamental
limitation in representing BCS components. This enables
building control engineers to define sensors, actuators, and
controllers directly within the BIM environment, creating a
unified semantic foundation for subsequent transformation
steps. The framework then leverages MDE principles to extract
structured representations from these enriched BIM models
and automatically generate deployable DT artifacts with minimal manual intervention.

Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime

It accurately describes the enrichment of BIM with BCS semantics, the use of MDE to extract and generate artifacts, and the integration of Eclipse Hono and Ditto for real-time data synchronization. Quotes: 'leverages Building Information Modeling (BIM) as a semantic backbone', 'systematically extracts relevant asset data from a BIM model and generates runtime interfaces', 'integrates Eclipse Hono and Ditto for real-time data exchange'.

The description correctly identifies the roles involved (Building Control Engineer, Facility Manager), the modeling of actuators within BIM, and the remote control interface. It also accurately notes the absence of specific technical details like ranges and safety constraints in the provided text. Quotes: 'define sensors, actuators, and controllers directly within the BIM environment', 'Facility Manager ... operates the physical infrastructure via the DT', 'generates runtime interfaces for bidirectional communication'.

The extraction accurately captures the semantic modeling of sensors in BIM and the real-time synchronization layer. However, it includes 'e.g., temperature, occupancy' as sensor types; while 'occupancy' is loosely implied by 'post-occupancy sensor data', 'temperature' is not explicitly mentioned in the provided source text for the framework's sensors. This represents a minor unsupported claim, slightly reducing the score. Quotes: 'define sensors ... directly within the BIM environment', 'post-occupancy sensor data', 'supports real-time data exchange ... continuous synchronization'.

The extraction is highly specific regarding the validation methodology, accurately describing the use of the Overall Digital Twin Entanglement (ODTE) metric to measure how well the DT reflects the physical asset. It correctly distinguishes between structural (BIM) and dynamic (real-time) aspects. Supported by: 'evaluate the entanglement of the resulting DT using the Overall Digital Twin Entanglement (ODTE) metric', 'measuring how well the resulting DT reflects its physical counterpart'.

block_4_docs_retrieved:
Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime



configuration management further enhances adaptability by
enabling adjustments without requiring full redeployment.
Complementing these functional requirements, our nonfunctional requirements ( **NF** _X_ ) address critical aspects of
interoperability across heterogeneous systems, scalability from
individual components to entire infrastructure systems, maintainability through low-overhead updates, and accessibility for
users with varying technical expertise.
This requirements-driven approach provides a systematic
foundation for implementing DTs that bridge the gap between
BIM’s static representations and the dynamic operational
needs of the AECO domain, addressing the challenges identified in Section II. At this, we want to emphasize that these
are not all requirements, but rather those pertinent to crafting a
technological spike solution tailored to the specific challenges
and innovations explored in this work (cf. Section V-C). The
discussion section will revisit these requirements to evaluate
their effectiveness and role in advancing the proposed solution.


_B. Solution Proposal_


Figure 1 (p. 6) presents the conceptual model of our
framework, illustrating how the requirements from Table III
are implemented through coordinated roles and system components. Figure 1 demonstrates the flow from enriched BIM
models to operational DTs, emphasizing the key roles and their
contributions to the DT life cycle.
_Building Control Engineers_, using BIM tools, perform
device modeling and initiate the deployment process. _Facility_
_Managers_ then interact with the deployed DT for real-time
monitoring and control. _DT Engineers_ extend the framework’s
capabilities through service integration. The DT Framework
acts as a central hub, facilitating bidirectional data exchange
and enabling a holistic approach to building life cycle management.


IV. M ODEL  - BASED D EPLOYMENT OF D IGITAL T WINS

block_6_docs_retrieved:
[11]. Most frameworks are not designed with AECO-specific
standards like BIM in mind, and those that can be adapted
— such as Bentley’s iTwin.js [32] — rely on proprietary
ecosystems or require substantial manual effort due to a lack
of automation.

Recently, Gerber et al. [33] presented an ad-hoc DT for
a Living Lab for a LEED Platinum academic building based
on BIM for monitoring, simulation, and performance analysis.
Their study emphasizes automated data acquisition, normalization, and 3D visualization, while highlighting challenges
in data integration and stakeholder coordination, insights that
directly inform model-driven approaches in DT engineering.
Their results reinforce earlier findings by Opoku et al. [34]
who also demonstrated the effective integration of DTs with
BIM and IoT platforms to optimize indoor environmental conditions in a university library setting. Both studies underscore
the significance of real-time data utilization and visualization
in enhancing facility management and highlight common challenges in data integration and stakeholder engagement in DT
implementations. In synopsis, AECO adoption is dominated by
commercial tools or custom-built solutions [2], [13]. Notably,
MDE is not present across the surveyed works, leaving a
critical gap in abstraction and reuse capabilities essential for
complex domains like AECO.
Model-driven engineering (MDE) offers a streamlined approach to handling the increasing complexity of systems by
elevating models to primary engineering artifacts [35], [36].
It enables abstraction, formalization, and automation across
the development life cycle, reducing cognitive overload and
improving consistency in large-scale systems [37]. MDE supports the definition of domain-specific modeling languages
and metamodels that unify engineering workflows, providing
a structured foundation for communication across disciplines
and life cycle phases [38], [39]. Importantly, MDE also
strengthens governance by encoding organizational policies
and architectural decisions into formal models, promoting
compliance, reuse, and quality assurance [40]. Recent advances continue to refine the integration of MDE into DT
systems [41]–[47]. As reported in Lehner et al.’s recent survey,
research primarily focuses on runtime adaptability, semantic
coherence, and tool interoperability [48]. Abbasi et al. [49]
examined the phenomenon of _semantic drift_, where the alignment between digital models and their physical counterparts
diverges over time. Their study stresses the necessity of
synchronization techniques to ensure the integrity of modelbased DTs. Kamburjan et al. [50] proposed a declarative life
cycle specification language, enabling dynamic evolution and
rule-based management of DT instances. Meanwhile, Oakes et
al. [51] advanced ontological frameworks to support service
